justification: O(n) dual-Set algorithm explicitly chosen over sorting and nested loops. AC4 correctly returns each duplicate exactly once via Set semantics, and the AC4 test covers IDs appearing 3+ times. All 5 ACs are mapped in acceptance_criteria_coverage. The deviation section explains why O(n²) would fail (~10,000ms at scale) and why the dual-Set approach was selected. The confidence section notes AC3 could flake on constrained CI runners, acknowledging it is a runtime characteristic rather than a pure unit-test concern.

observations.text: Dual-Set approach is genuinely O(n) and arguably more efficient than a Map-based counter for this use case
AC4 edge case explicitly tested with IDs appearing 3+ times, returned exactly once
Includes actual benchmark data (~67ms for 1M IDs) demonstrating AC3 compliance
Deviation section transparently explains the 200ms Jest threshold vs 100ms spec requirement
Extra edge-case tests beyond requirements: single element, all identical, multiple distinct duplicates

Confidence section notes AC3 flakiness risk but does not explicitly state that O(n) complexity cannot be verified by a unit test — it frames it as a CI stability concern rather than a fundamental limitation of unit testing runtime characteristics

## Implementation Summary

### What was built
`findDuplicateOrderIds(orderIds: string[]): string[]` — an O(n) TypeScript function that returns each ID appearing more than once, exactly once.

### Approach
**Dual-Set algorithm** — one `Set<string>` (`seen`) tracks every ID encountered; a second (`dups`) accumulates IDs the moment they appear a second time. A single `for…of` loop handles both. Result is `[...dups]`.

- No sorting required → avoids
